data.title: POET Technologies: The Recent $75Mn Fundraising Is A Stamp Of Validation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Let me start by saying that POET Technologies (NASDAQ:POET) is sitting in the sweet spot of a once in a generation tech boom. The company's optical interposer technology addresses a critical bottleneck in AI data centers. That is moving huge amounts of data at high speed and low power using light instead of copper. I have previously rated POET as a strong buy, and the stock has rewarded my conviction by soaring 83% and 15%, respectively. And despite the recent slowdown, including the stock taking and the quant ratings moving from strong buy to strong sell, I still believe that POET's story is just getting started. Therefore, I am maintaining my strong Buy rating. And here are some of my reasons.

First and foremost, the company has a unique technology solving a big problem. POET's patented optical interposer platform allows seamless integration of electronics and photonic devices on a single chip. This is to mean that it lets data centers replace short distance copper connections with optical links that are cheaper, faster, and more power-efficient. And this is exactly what hyperscalers such as Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) need as AI workloads continue to explode.

Secondly, there is a huge demand for AI compute, which is outpacing capacity. For instance both Microsoft and Amazon (AMZN) have actually openly said that they can't keep up with the demand for AI, and big tech plans to spend hundreds of billions of dollars on AI infrastructure. Optical transceiver sales are projected to grow 56.5% annually, reaching 31.9M high speed units in 2025. And POET's optical engines are aimed squarely at this booming market and next-gen applications like chip-to-chip optical links.

Thirdly, I would say that POET is now well funded. This year, POET flipped from R&D mode to commercial mode. It reported its first product revenues and Non Recurring engineering [NRE] revenue in 2025. The revenues might be seen as quite small at $0.27M, but it's proof that customers are now paying for its solutions. The company has actually secured major manufacturing partners and raised a war chest of $150Mn in cash with zero debt. And this includes the recent $75Mn private placement in October. This capital enables POET to scale R&D, expand production, and even pursue strategic acquisitions without financial strain.

Lastly, I believe POET has blue chips customers and partnerships. POET isn't toiling in a vacuum. It has a Tier 1 tech manufacturer on board. Its largest customers include Foxconn's network arm (OTCPK:FXCOF) and Luxshare Tech, who are co-developing the 800G and 1.6T optical transceiver modules using POET's engine. It has also partnered with Mitsubishi Electric (OTCPK:MIELY) to integrate lasers into its design. And it's also just added Lessengers, which is a South Korean Optical module maker, as a new customer committed to an 800G module project. I believe these relationships validate POET's tech and give it channels into hyperscale data centers via well-connected suppliers.

Growth and Profitability

POET is still in the Pre-commercial revenue stage, but it's on the cusp of a sharp inflection. In the latest quarter, the company recorded $268K of revenue. That's essentially negligible revenue, but the growth rate was infinite, and it signals that 2025 will be the first year with meaningful sales. In fact, over the past year POET's revenues grew significantly, reflecting the start of product shipments. And while the absolute numbers are tiny, the trend matters; POET is transitioning from technology development to product development, as management puts it. And this is the moment investors have been waiting for.

When I look into the future, I believe POET's growth will be driven by the AI spending boom. And this is because the sheer scale of investments going into AI infrastructure is hard to fathom; the US government's $500B Stargate project, Big Tech planned $325B, Amazon alone $100B for its data centers. And all that money means more high-speed connections between AI servers. Optical transceivers of 400G, 800G and 1.6T speeds are flying off the shelves. According to TrendForce, transceivers are expected to reach 31.9Mn by the end of 2025.

When it comes to profitability, I won't sugarcoat the profitability picture. POET is not going to turn a profit in 2025 or likely 2026. And this is because these are the early days. The company lost $17Mn last quarter and will continue to burn cash as it scales. Gross margins, however, are expected to be attractive once sales ramp, because the optical interposer design lowers component and assembly costs. The company has actually stated that its products "are lower cost, consume less power ... and are readily scalable to high volumes." And this implies that POET can price competitively yet still enjoy solid margins at scale. I would say that for now, the focus is top-line growth, and in my view, that's absolutely the right strategy. Gain share in this landgrab phase, and then profitability will follow if you become a key player in a multibillion-dollar market.

Growth Catalysts

The biggest catalyst for POET is the unprecedented buildout of AI data centers that is happening globally. We're in an AI arms race, and one bottleneck that's emerged is connecting all those GPUs and servers. It's not just about having the fastest chips because those chips need to talk to each other at Terabit speed. And that's where optical engines come in. At the risk of sounding grandiose, POET is operating in what I believe could be a generational shift from electrical to optical connectivity inside data centers.

What actually strikes me is that Microsoft's CEO openly said that their data centers are constrained, and on the other hand, Amazon is pouring $100Bn to alleviate network bottlenecks. And of course this has shattered forecasts for optics demand. Transceiver unit demand is now on a trajectory that was unthinkable a couple of years ago. And every one of those high-speed modules needs optical engines and light sources, which are POET's core products.

Then there is the $75Mn private placement boost, which is not just a cash infusion. In fact, it is a stamp of validation. A single institutional investor was willing to write a $75Mn check, which is the largest in POET's history. To me, this fundraising is a catalyst in two ways: One is that it gives POET the means to accelerate its business operations through R&D, hiring, tech, and so on, and secondly, it increases visibility because big money is now involved, which often precedes big developments.

Valuation

By conventional metrics, POET's stock is extremely expensive. With an $800Mn market cap and practically no trailing revenue, POET's price-to-sales (P/S) is in the hundreds, as shown below.

For context, established optical components peers trade at single-digit sales multiples. For instance, Coherent Corp (COHR) and Ciena (CIEN) both in single digits. For COHR its FWD Price/sales stands at 3.01x, while the sector median stands at 3.56x, as shown below.

On the other side, Ciena has its FWD Price/sales at 5.19x, while the sector median is at 3.56x. These are obviously higher than POET's 480.41x

When I look at the broader communications equipment sector, like Lumentum (LITE), I note that its FWD P/S stands at 4.95x.

So by any relative measure, POET stock carries a hefty premium. However, this rich valuation is the price of admission for the potential payoff. I am not buying POET for what it is today but for what it could become in 2-5 years. The market is valuing POET less like a hardware manufacturer and more like an IP-driven, high-growth tech platform. But at the end of the day, the valuation is high because the growth expectations are quite high.

POET's Risks

The biggest risk I see for POET is execution risk. POET must successfully transition from development to large scale production and sales. And this means scaling manufacturing without hiccups, meeting customer specs, and hitting delivery timelines. Therefore, any missteps and delays in the new Malaysia facility could frustrate customers and give competing solutions an opening.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, POET Technologies presents a high-risk, high-reward investment opportunity. It's not a blue chip. It's not profitable. And it's valued on a dream of future dominance in a nascent market. And this is why I am giving the company a strong buy rating because I believe POET has a real shot at becoming a critical enabler in the AI infrastructure ecosystem.

data.title: Garrett Motion Took Shareholders On A Heck Of A Ride
data.description:
October 23rd was a fantastic day for shareholders of Garrett Motion Inc. (NASDAQ:GTX). Shares of the company skyrocketed 19.3% higher after management announced a stunning revenue and earnings beat. This surge is great to see, and it served as justification for management to increase revenue and profit guidance for 2025 across the board.

This comes after a rather difficult time that the company had been exhibiting, with declining revenue from 2023 to 2024, and mixed bottom line results. It appears as though the worst for the company is now behind it. And of course, what really excites me about this is the fact that shares of the company are still incredibly cheap, both on an absolute basis and compared to most other similar enterprises. When you add on top of this certain initiatives that management is working on, it's clear to me that this makes for a very solid Buy candidate, even at a time when there is uncertainty in this industry caused by broader economic issues.

Taking Garrett Motion for a test drive

Before I get into the exact financial performance that Garrett Motion achieved during its quarter, I think it is necessary to dive a bit into exactly what the company does. Management describes this as a provider of differentiated solutions dedicated to the emission reduction and energy efficiency activities in the mobility and industrial spaces. I know this is very vague. So additional detail is warranted.

Specifically, the company produces engineered turbocharging technologies, air and fluid compression technologies, and high speed electric motor technologies for its customers. These offerings are utilized in the production of light vehicles, commercial vehicles, and various industrial applications.

While this is a diverse player that touches on both the mobility and industrial spaces, it would be wise to know that most of the firm is dedicated to servicing the passenger vehicle market. In fact, 69% of its revenue in 2024 came from that space. The other 31% was on the industrial side, with the company producing turbochargers for highway vehicles like trucks and buses, those same products for agricultural, construction, mining, and marine equipment, and certain parts and services for the commercial vehicle aftermarket.

In recent years, the company has seen some rather mixed financial performance. From 2022 to 2023, revenue popped from $3.60 billion to $3.89 billion. This was driven by strength across most of its product categories. But the largest, by far, was its Gas product line, which reported a jump in revenue from $1.49 billion to $1.72 billion. This jump in sales can be attributed to the fact that, the year before, there was a global semiconductor shortage and certain lockdown measures in China aimed at combating COVID-19.

In essence, you could argue that the previous year, 2022, had been held back by these issues. On top of this, the company benefited from new product launches and the ramp-up of additional production capabilities across all of its regions.

For last year, revenue did drop, falling to $3.48 billion. There were two main drivers in this case. Once again, the Gas product line of the company played a role, with revenue dropping to $1.51 billion. Management blamed this on softer demand in both China and North America that more than offset strength in Europe, other parts of Asia, and South America. In addition to this, the Diesel product line for the company reported a decline in revenue, with sales dropping from $992 million to $827 million. This decline was driven by lower demand in the passenger vehicle market in Europe.

It's really difficult to understand exactly all of the aspects of global economic conditions that played a role in these fluctuations. But we do know that, in the U.S. and China for instance, consumer confidence levels are near all time lows. In the U.S., this seems to stem from inflationary pressures and the high interest rates aimed at combating them. In a previous article, I talked some about China and its economy. In short, a downturn in the property sector, which is a huge source of wealth for citizens there, and the shock felt from COVID-19 related lockdowns, have come home to roost.

The property market issue in China seems to be the result of years of over investment in the space, which included significant amounts of debt. And now with China's population starting to decline, this will continue to weigh on the country to some extent in the future. Considering that 20.1% of the company's revenue last year came from the US, while another 18.5% came from China, what goes on in those regions is important to the business. And in Europe, which accounts for 47.3% to the company's revenue, the same issues that affected the U.S., as well as the cut off of certain incentives for electric vehicle purchases, as an example, proved problematic.

Has been quite volatile as well. As the first chart in this article illustrates, net income, operating cash flow, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA have all been a bit volatile. But considering how things have gone in the industry, any impact demand changes have caused, this does not surprise me whatsoever. In fact, I would have been surprised if profits and cash flows had continued growing throughout the volatility.

This year is really shaping up!

One thing that the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year did for shareholders was to prove that the worst for Garrett Motion is now behind it. During that quarter, revenue for the company came in at $902 million. That was 9.2% above the $826 million the company reported a year earlier. This jump in revenue was driven by strength across most of its product lines. This included its Gas operations that expanded from $360 million to $405 million. Program ramp-ups in Europe, China, India, and North America were instrumental in pushing revenue up, and the company also benefited from new application launches during the quarter.

We also saw growth in Diesel, with sales expanding from $180 million to $201 million thanks to a $9 million benefit from foreign currency fluctuations and strong light commercial vehicle demand in Europe. Improvements when it came to pickup trucks in North America, South America, and Southeast Asia, all helped as well.

And finally, the Commercial Vehicle operations of the company grew from $155 million to $164 million. That can be attributed to greater demand in Europe when it came to on-highway offerings. Despite challenges remaining in Europe, such as inflationary pressures and high interest rates, consumers are expressing some optimism right now.

It is important to note that much of the improvement the company has seen appears to be from market share growth and the launch of new products. For instance, in the second quarter of this year, the company said that it is seeing growing interest in turbochargers for range-extended electric vehicles. Plus in the second quarter of this year, it also had a major win in the light vehicle turbocharger program in the form of a contract extension valued at around $1 billion.

Management also noted for this quarter that they achieved awards for turbochargers for hybrids. Plus, in addition to this, the company saw over $40 million of awards that support stationary power generation. All of these, as well as the aforementioned range extended electric vehicles part of the business, should push revenue up by over $100 million this year alone. All of these made possible revenue that ended up being about $35.8 million greater than what analysts were hoping to see. Moving forward, the company also seems to be making good progress on 0 emission products, including proof of concepts for advancements in E-Cooling technology, as well as for those involving high speed 3-in-1 E-Powertrain offerings.

The quarter saw earnings per share of $0.38. That's well above the $0.24 in earnings reported a year earlier. In fact, the company ended up exceeding analysts’ expectations here by $0.05. This showing allowed overall net profits to jump from $52 million to $77 million. Other profitability metrics grew also. Operating cash flow shot up from $67 million to $100 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $97 million to $104 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business grew from $144 million to $164 million.

In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. As was the case in the third quarter of the year, revenue, profits, and cash flows, all expanded nicely.

The quarter was so strong, in fact, that management has even increased guidance for this year. In the image above, you can see precisely what I mean. In fact, based on this guidance, we should probably expect adjusted operating cash flow of about $432 million. That would also be up from what the company saw in 2024 when it delivered $425 million. I know that's not a huge gain, but considering how last year went, I see this as a win.

If we use these estimates provided by management, and we also use the historical results that the company achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above exactly how the stock is valued. These are very low multiples on an absolute basis. But even better than that, they are low compared to other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked Garrett Motion up against five other similar enterprises. On a price to earnings basis, it was the cheapest of the group. And when it came to the other two valuation approaches, only one of the firms was cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Garrett Motion 11.0 7.1 6.9
Dana Incorporated (DAN) 134.8 8.8 8.7
Dorman Products (DORM) 21.2 27.2 13.1
Lear Corp (LEA) 11.8 5.4 4.9
Gentex Corp (GNTX) 14.9 10.7 10.0
Gentherm (THRM) 36.6 10.0 7.3

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I'm quite optimistic about Garrett Motion. It has been a bumpy ride, but the firm has done an exceedingly good job. New product launches should help to propel additional revenue and profit growth. Cash flows should follow that same trajectory. Add on top of this how cheap GTX stock is, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies, and I have no reason to be anything other than bullish right now.

data.title: Existing Home Sales Rise 1.5% To 7-Month High
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Existing home sales rose 1.5% in September to their highest level in seven months. According to the National Association of Realtors (NAR), existing home sales were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.06 million units, as expected. Existing home sales are up 4.1% compared to one year ago.

"As anticipated, falling mortgage rates are lifting home sales," said NAR Chief Economist Dr. Lawrence Yun. "Improving housing affordability is also contributing to the increase in sales."

"Inventory is matching a five-year high, though it remains below pre-COVID levels," Yun added. "Many homeowners are financially comfortable, resulting in very few distressed properties and forced sales. Home prices continue to rise in most parts of the country, further contributing to overall household wealth."

(Press release)

Background on Existing Home Sales

Existing home sales measures the monthly sales of previously owned single-family homes. It makes up a huge part of the residential real estate market, as 90% of purchased homes are previously owned according to the National Association of Realtors.

More importantly, there exists a strong correlation between purchases of existing homes and consumer spending. An increase in existing home sales can indirectly stimulate economic activity with increased consumer spending on new furnishings and appliances. Alternatively, a sustained drop in existing home sales often foreshadows a downturn in the economy.

Here is a snapshot of the data series, which comes from the National Association of Realtors. The data since January 1999 was previously available in the St. Louis Fed's FRED repository and is now only available for the last twelve months.

Over this time frame, we clearly see the real estate bubble, which peaked in 2005 and then fell dramatically. Sales were volatile for the first year or so following the Great Recession, with monthly sales as low as 3.45 million units and as high as 5.44 million units. We have seen that same volatility following the most recent recession, with sales ranging from 3.85 million units to 6.73 million units.

Existing Home Sales: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Now let's examine the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show a 23.3% increase in the US population since the turn of the century. The snapshot below is an overlay of the NAR's annualized estimates with a population-adjusted version.

Existing home sales are 22.4% below the NAR's January 2000 estimate. The population-adjusted version is 36.3% below the turn-of-the-century sales.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage in September 2025 was 6.35%, the lowest level in a year. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

Existing Home Sales: Median Price

The median price for existing homes fell for a third straight month in August, coming in at $415,200. This represents a 1.7% decline from the previous month and a 2.1% increase from one year ago, marking the 27th consecutive month of year-over-year increases.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

data.title: Zegona Communications: Why I Have Been Trimming
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alluvial Fund Third Quarter 2025 Letter To Partners

Our largest position continues to be Zegona Communications (OTC:ZEGLF) and my, what a year the company has had! In August, Zegona announced a binding agreement to sell a portion of its fiber optic network joint venture to Singapore's sovereign wealth fund. When completed this quarter, the transaction will result in a large cash inflow for Zegona, enabling it to distribute a meaningful special dividend and effectively redeem the preferred shares it issued to Vodafone (VOD) in the Vodafone Spain buyout. Zegona continues to work toward monetizing another fiber optic joint venture, which should result in another large cash inflow. Together, these transactions substantially derisk the Zegona story. When we first invested, we were buying into a leveraged buyout situation where value creation was dependent on deleveraging through asset sales. Though I considered it unlikely, the process could have been derailed by skeptical regulators, jittery capital markets, or any number of macroeconomic troubles. Management has come through for shareholders in a big way with these asset sales. These transactions crystallize the value embedded in Zegona's fiber optic network, normalizing the balance sheet and allowing Zegona to focus on operational improvements.

"Multiple arbitrage" is a well-known corporate strategy where a company trading at, say, 10x earnings attempts to buy companies for 5-8x earnings, hoping the market will continue to capitalize the resulting combined earnings at 10x. Instant value creation! Happily, it also works in reverse. The market values Zegona at about 6x cash flow, but the company keeps on finding assets it can sell for 12x or more. Zegona is rumored to be considering a sale of its mobile network infrastructure in conjunction with competitor MasOrange, and may also be considering a sale of data centers it acquired with Vodafone Spain. Either transaction would result in material additional cash proceeds that could be used for deleveraging, investment in growth and efficiencies, or returned to shareholders.

Despite their excellent performance year-to-date, I continue to see upside of 50% or more in Zegona shares. The company's efforts to return to subscriber growth and improve margins should bear fruit in 2026, and further asset sales and return of capital announcements are potential catalysts.

I have been trimming our Zegona position, simply for the purposes of risk control. I think a lot about what I term "asteroid risk," the possibility that some completely unforeseeable negative event, such as an asteroid obliterating company headquarters, could cause an investment to permanently impaired. My job as portfolio manager is to ensure Alluvial Fund is never so exposed to a particular security that an unforeseeable event, be it celestial or biological in origin, could leave a smoking crater in our portfolio. Sometimes this means selling shares of fundamentally sound companies simply because they have grown to a size that exceeds sensible risk limits.

Disclosures

Investment in Alluvial Fund are subject to risk, including the risk of permanent loss. Alluvial Fund's strategy may experience greater volatility and drawdowns than market indexes. An investment in Alluvial Fund is not intended to be a complete investment program and is not intended for short-term investment. Before investing, potential limited partners should carefully evaluate their financial situation and their ability to tolerate volatility. Alluvial Capital Management, LLC believes the figures, calculations and statistics included in this letter to be correct but provides no warranty against errors in calculation or transcription. Alluvial Capital Management, LLC is a Registered Investment Advisor. This communication does not constitute a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any investment securities.

Performance Notes

Net performance figures are for a typical limited partner under the standard fee arrangement. Returns for partners' capital accounts may vary depending on individual fee arrangements. Alluvial Fund, LP has a fiscal year end of December 31, 2024 and is subject to an annual audit by Cohen & Company. Performance figures for year-to-date periods are calculated by NAV Consulting, Inc. Year-to-date figures are unaudited and are subject to change. Gross performance figures are reported net of all partnership expenses. data.title: 7 Stocks Taking The Next Step To Be On Offense
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Longleaf Partners Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Bio-Rad - Bio-Rad Laboratories (BIO), a healthcare products manufacturer, was a positive contributor after the company reported steadier earnings than the market expected in a difficult environment. This sets the company up for the ability to grow earnings in 2026 and beyond, more in-line with previous industry trends. The company's important investment in Sartorius (OTCPK:SARTF) (OTCPK:SARTF) has also shown encouraging year-over-year trends following a difficult couple of years, and we are getting closer to a period when both companies will have increased strategic flexibility. In the meantime, Bio-Rad continues to be a material share repurchaser with its nearly net cash balance sheet.

PVH (PVH) - Branded apparel company PVH performed well in the quarter as the company continued to demonstrate that while quarterly earnings could have some volatility, the long-run earnings per share power of this company remains intact at over $10 per share. After hiccups at the Calvin Klein brand earlier this year, the company has gotten it back on track. PVH also remains our largest share repurchaser for the year after buying a teens percentage of shares outstanding at great prices. While this is a company that is sensitive to consumer trends, its brands resonate with a more price conscious buyer, plus the company still has numerous levers to pull to improve margins.

Rayonier (RYN) and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) - We initiated positions in both Rayonier and PotlatchDeltic in the quarter. Rayonier was an immediate contributor. We discuss it more above in combination with PotlatchDeltic, as the two companies announced an accretive merger after quarter end. Southeastern has a strong long-term history investing in timberland assets, including these two companies multiple times before. This has been a tough year for housing-related stocks, and these companies have been no exception. That said, both have been on offense with accretive share repurchase and asset sales to capture the wide gap between private and public market valuations. Now they are taking the next step to be on offense by joining forces. We expect the combined company to be our largest position, which is exciting given its combination of Business, People and Price.

Albertsons (ACI) - We wrote about supermarket operator Albertsons earlier this year as a contributor in tougher stock market times. We took some off the table then but added back to our position in the third quarter on weakness. The stock was down on a quarterly earnings report that did not excite the market but was within our expectations. We were further pleased to see meaningful share repurchase at a high single digit annualized pace of shares outstanding. Later in the quarter, Albertsons' industry was pressured by news of Amazon's (AMZN) increased efforts in groceries. While we will never dismiss Amazon, we would also point out that this latest push is more of an incremental, expected move than something like the surprising acquisition of Whole Foods. This remains a competitive industry, but it is also a steadily necessary one in which Albertsons has a strong position thanks to its great real estate, brand recognition and owner alignment. Our confidence was bolstered further after quarter end when the company reported another solid set of results and announced a sizeable accelerated share repurchase, further growing value per share.

Fidelity National Information Services (FIS) - Financial software provider FIS was a detractor for the quarter. The Capital Markets segment grew 5%, which was less than expectations. Management cited volumes rebounding in Q3, but the market is skeptical. Margins were also slightly lower than expected due to an acquisition with lower margins that should revert to higher margins as it is integrated. We believe FIS' underlying fundamentals remain on-track, and the upcoming purchase of Global Payment's Issuer Solutions business and sale of FIS' remaining Worldpay stake will further improve FIS' economic predictability and increase FCF conversion. In the interim, management continues to repurchase stock and recently increased the 2025 repurchase plan by $100 million to $1.3 billion citing "the share price is just too low. So, we see it as a value."

Mattel (MAT) - Children's toy, media, and consumer products creator Mattel was a detractor for the quarter. Second quarter sales were below expectations as North American retailers adjusted ordering to domestic shipping vs. importing directly. This was to buy time to see if tariff rates abated, which resulted in a two-month lag in sales recognition. This resulted in North American sales down 16% while international segment sales increased 7%. Point-of-sale sell through at retailers was positive for Q2 and YTD showing healthy underlying demand as toys are somewhat non-discretionary. Mattel continues to execute operationally with improved gross margins even with lower-than-expected sales. While we believe 2025 will show overall modest sales growth, 2026 should accelerate given two new movies and at least one new digital game being launched in addition to a solid partner movie slate where Mattel produces related toys. In the interim, management is using almost all its FCF to repurchase shares at depressed prices. They plan to repurchase $340 million in 2H25 which equates to over 5% of shares outstanding.

See following page for important disclosures.

Before investing in any Longleaf Partners Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For a current Prospectus and Summary Prospectus, which contain this and other important information, visit Longleaf# . Please read the Prospectus and Summary Prospectus carefully before investing.

RISKS

The Longleaf Partners Fund is subject to stock market risk, meaning stocks in the Fund may fluctuate in response to developments at individual companies or due to general market and economic conditions. Also, because the Fund generally invests in 15 to 25 companies, share value could fluctuate more than if a greater number of securities were held. Mid-cap stocks held by the Fund may be more volatile than those of larger companies.

The S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index is an index of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity and industry grouping, among other factors. The S&P is designed to be a leading indicator of U.S. equities and is meant to reflect the risk/return characteristics of the large cap universe. S&P 500 Value Index constituents are drawn from the S&P 500 and are based on three factors: the ratios of book value, earnings, and sales to price. An index is unmanaged, does not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and cannot be invested in directly.

The Russell 1000 Index measures the performance of the 1,000 largest companies in the Russell 3000 Index, which represents approximately 90% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000 Index. The Russell 1000 Value index is drawn from the constituents of the Russell 1000 based on book-to-price (B/P) ratio. An index is unmanaged, does not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and cannot be invested in directly.

P/V ("price to value") is a calculation that compares the prices of the stocks in a portfolio to Southeastern's appraisal of their intrinsic values. The ratio represents a single data point about a Fund and should not be construed as something more. P/V does not guarantee future results, and we caution investors not to give this calculation undue weight.

"Margin of Safety" is a reference to the difference between a stock's market price and Southeastern's calculated appraisal value. It is not a guarantee of investment performance or returns.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) is a measure of a company's ability to generate the cash flow necessary to maintain operations. Generally, it is calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.

Price / Earnings (P/E) is the ratio of a company's share price compared to its earnings per share.

PE multiple is a financial metric that frames a company's current stock price in terms of the company's earnings per share.

EBITDA is a company's earnings before interest, taxes depreciation and amortization.

The Magnificent 7 stocks are a group of large-cap companies (corporations with large market capitalizations determined by the number of shares times each share's value) in the technology sector, including Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) (parent company of Google (GOOG, GOOGL) (GOOG, GOOGL)), Amazon, Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META) (parent company of Facebook and Instagram), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA). Due to their size and performance, these stocks accounted for roughly one-third of the S&P 500's total market capitalization at the end of 2024.

As of September 30, 2025, the top ten holdings for the Longleaf Partners Fund: CNX Resources (CNX), 6.2%; Mattel, 5.8%; Kraft Heinz (KHC), 5.8%, IAC (IAC), 5.6%; EXOR, 5.6%; FedEx (FDX), 5.5%; Albertsons, 5.3%: Undisclosed, 5.2%; Regeneron (REGN), 4.9% and PVH, 4.8%. Fund holdings are subject to change and holdings discussions are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.
